pathways.summary: A charge dismissed or resulting in acquittal is sealed automatically at disposition. No application; confirm via DCJS. Five mechanisms, considered in routing order. ruleClauses: A charge dismissed or resulting in acquittal is sealed
automatically at disposition. No application; confirm via DCJS.
Five mechanisms, considered in routing order.
Non-conviction sealing (CPL 160.50 / 160.55)
When a case ends in the defendant's favor — dismissal, acquittal, declined prosecution, an adjournment in
contemplation of dismissal (ACD) that is dismissed, or a vacated/overturned conviction — the record is
SEALED automatically under CPL 160.50. Where the case ends in a non-criminal VIOLATION or traffic
infraction, CPL 160.55 provides a more limited sealing. No application is needed; the court directs sealing at
Marijuana destruction / expungement (MRTA)
The one true erasure in New York. Convictions for unlawful possession of marijuana and fifth-degree criminal
possession of marijuana are expunged/destroyed automatically under the MRTA. A person may also request
destruction of those records through OCA. Expunged marijuana convictions do NOT count toward the
two-conviction limit under CPL 160.59.
Conditional / treatment sealing (CPL 160.58)
A person convicted of certain drug or specified offenses who successfully completes a judicial-diversion or
court-approved substance-abuse treatment program, and all other sentence terms, may have those convictions
conditionally sealed. The sealing can be reopened on a subsequent arrest, and resealed if that arrest does not
lead to conviction. This pathway is tied to treatment completion rather than a flat waiting period.
Discretionary sealing by petition (CPL 160.59)
Enacted 2017. A person with a limited history may petition the sentencing court to seal up to TWO convictions,
no more than ONE of which is a felony, after at least 10 years have passed since sentencing or release
(whichever is later). Crimes from a single criminal transaction may be treated as one. The offense must be an
“eligible offense” (Section 3). Grant is discretionary; the court weighs rehabilitation and the interests of justice.
Automatic Clean Slate sealing (CPL 160.57)
Effective Nov 16, 2024. Eligible New York State convictions seal automatically after a waiting period — 3
years for misdemeanors, 8 years for felonies — measured from sentencing or release, whichever is later, with
no conviction-free pending charges. No application is required. OCA/DCJS have until Nov 16, 2027 to seal the
pre-existing back-catalog, so many records are pending.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules
sourceSections.text:
Five mechanisms, considered in routing order.

Mechanism 1 — Non-conviction sealing (CPL 160.50 / 160.55)
When a case ends in the defendant's favor — dismissal, acquittal, declined prosecution, an adjournment in
contemplation of dismissal (ACD) that is dismissed, or a vacated/overturned conviction — the record is
SEALED automatically under CPL 160.50. Where the case ends in a non-criminal VIOLATION or traffic
infraction, CPL 160.55 provides a more limited sealing. No application is needed; the court directs sealing at
disposition.

Mechanism 2 — Marijuana destruction / expungement (MRTA)
The one true erasure in New York. Convictions for unlawful possession of marijuana and fifth-degree criminal
possession of marijuana are expunged/destroyed automatically under the MRTA. A person may also request
destruction of those records through OCA. Expunged marijuana convictions do NOT count toward the
two-conviction limit under CPL 160.59.

Mechanism 3 — Conditional / treatment sealing (CPL 160.58)
A person convicted of certain drug or specified offenses who successfully completes a judicial-diversion or
court-approved substance-abuse treatment program, and all other sentence terms, may have those convictions
conditionally sealed. The sealing can be reopened on a subsequent arrest, and resealed if that arrest does not
lead to conviction. This pathway is tied to treatment completion rather than a flat waiting period.

Mechanism 4 — Discretionary sealing by petition (CPL 160.59)
Enacted 2017. A person with a limited history may petition the sentencing court to seal up to TWO convictions,
no more than ONE of which is a felony, after at least 10 years have passed since sentencing or release
(whichever is later). Crimes from a single criminal transaction may be treated as one. The offense must be an
“eligible offense” (Section 3). Grant is discretionary; the court weighs rehabilitation and the interests of justice.

Mechanism 5 — Automatic Clean Slate sealing (CPL 160.57)
Effective Nov 16, 2024. Eligible New York State convictions seal automatically after a waiting period — 3
years for misdemeanors, 8 years for felonies — measured from sentencing or release, whichever is later, with
no conviction-free pending charges. No application is required. OCA/DCJS have until Nov 16, 2027 to seal the
pre-existing back-catalog, so many records are pending.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Route by disposition and offense. Non-conviction → already sealed (160.50/160.55). Marijuana
possession → MRTA destruction (true erasure). Drug conviction after treatment → 160.58. Other eligible
convictions → Clean Slate automatic (3/8 yr) or a 160.59 petition to seal sooner / reach what Clean Slate
doesn't.
Be precise: except MRTA marijuana, NY “clears” records by SEALING, not expungement — the record
persists and stays visible to law enforcement, courts, and certain licensing/firearms authorities.

(160.59) eligible offense

The two conviction-sealing routes differ sharply in timing and effort. Clean Slate is automatic and faster (3
years misdemeanor / 8 years felony) but limited to its eligible set and still phasing in. The CPL 160.59 petition
takes a longer 10-year wait and court approval but the person controls the timing and can file now rather than
wait for the automatic system. For Clean Slate, any new conviction or pending charge during the waiting period
restarts or blocks the clock.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Compute from sentencing or release, whichever is later. For a person between years 3/8 and 10, Clean
Slate (if eligible) is the faster route; if the offense is outside Clean Slate, the 160.59 10-year petition may
still reach it.
A new conviction or open charge resets/blocks both clocks — confirm no pending charges before
indicating eligibility.

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / ineligible offenses
sourceSections.text:
The exclusion lists differ between the discretionary petition (160.59) and Clean Slate (160.57). The agent must
apply the list matching the pathway.

sourceSections.title: Ineligible for CPL 160.59 discretionary sealing
sourceSections.text:
An “eligible offense” is any NY crime EXCEPT the following, which can never be sealed under 160.59:

• Sex offenses under Penal Law Article 130, and any offense requiring sex-offender registration (Correction
Law Art. 6-C).
• Child pornography / sexual performance by a child offenses under Article 263.
• Felony homicide offenses under Article 125.
• Violent felony offenses as defined in Penal Law § 70.02.
• Class A felonies.
• Felony conspiracy or attempt (Article 105 / attempts) where the underlying target offense is not itself eligible.

sourceSections.title: Ineligible for Clean Slate automatic sealing (160.57)
sourceSections.text:
• Sex offenses and any offense requiring sex-offender registration.
• Non-drug Class A felonies — including murder and other top-tier felonies. (Drug-related Class A felonies
under Penal Law Article 220 CAN still qualify.)
• Cases where the person received a life sentence.
• Any person with pending criminal charges — ineligible while a charge is open.
• Federal convictions and out-of-state convictions — Clean Slate reaches NY State convictions only.

Other categorical limits
• Two-conviction / one-felony cap (160.59). No more than two convictions, and no more than one felony,
may be sealed by petition.
• Mixed records. If a person has both eligible and ineligible convictions, the eligible ones may still be sealed;
the ineligible ones remain.
• Sealed records still count. A sealed conviction still counts as a prior for sentence-enhancement in any future
case (160.59(10)).

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The 160.59 “eligible offense” definition and the Clean Slate exclusions turn on the exact Penal
Law article and class of each conviction (e.g., whether a Class A felony is a drug offense under
Article 220). The agent must map each conviction to its Penal Law citation and class and verify
against the current statute before declaring it eligible — the offense label alone is not enough.
Note also the Brooklyn DA guidance that pre-2007 PL 265.02(4) weapon convictions are not
sealable.

AGENT GUIDANCE
The key Clean Slate nuance is the Class A felony split: non-drug Class A felonies are permanently
excluded, but Article 220 drug Class A felonies can qualify. Check the article, not just the class.
An open/pending charge disqualifies under both pathways — confirm the record is otherwise closed
before advising relief.

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Disposition and offense determine the mechanism and whether a filing is needed. This table is the agent's
primary routing logic for New York.

Case outcome Mechanism Route Standard / wait

Dismissed / acquitted / 160.50 Seal — automatic At disposition
declined / ACD dismissed

Convicted of non-criminal 160.55 Seal — automatic (limited) At disposition
violation/infraction

Marijuana possession MRTA Destroy — automatic None
conviction

Drug conviction + 160.58 Conditional seal — motion On completion
treatment completed

Eligible misdemeanor 160.57 / 160.59 Auto (3 yr) or petition (10 yr) No pending charges
conviction

Eligible felony conviction 160.57 / 160.59 Auto (8 yr) or petition (10 yr) ≤ 1 felony by petition

Excluded offense (Sec. 3) — Not sealable Categorical bar

Effect of relief: a sealed record does not appear on background checks by employers, housing providers, or
other non-law-enforcement entities, and the person may generally answer “no” to a criminal-record question.
But sealed records remain accessible to law enforcement, prosecutors, and courts in a new criminal case; to
fingerprint-based background checks for jobs involving children, the elderly, or vulnerable people; and for
police/peace-officer employment and gun licensing. Federal and immigration authorities may also access them.
Only the MRTA marijuana remedy actually destroys the record.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Tell users plainly that sealing is not erasure (except MRTA marijuana): the record persists and stays
visible to the enumerated authorities, and still counts as a prior for sentencing.
For someone with an immigration concern, flag that sealed records remain accessible to immigration
authorities and that sealing does not remove immigration consequences — recommend they consult an
immigration attorney.

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Only the discretionary (160.59) and conditional (160.58) pathways require a filing; 160.50/160.55 and Clean
Slate are automatic, and MRTA marijuana destruction is automatic (with an optional OCA destruction request).
The NY courts publish a statewide 160.59 application.

CPL 160.59 discretionary sealing
• Sealing Application (Notice of Motion and Affidavit in Support of Sealing) — the statewide NY Courts
form, sworn before a notary, identifying the conviction(s) and the reasons for sealing.
• Certificate(s) of Disposition — obtained from the court for each case to be sealed (the
case/docket/indictment number and court name come from it); a separate request per case.
• A sworn statement on whether other sealing applications have been or will be filed, and copies of any such
applications.

CPL 160.58 conditional sealing
• A motion in the sentencing court documenting successful completion of judicial diversion or the
court-approved treatment program and all other sentence terms.

Automatic / destruction pathways
• 160.50 / 160.55 and Clean Slate (160.57): no form — sealing is directed by the court / done by OCA-DCJS.
The person verifies via a DCJS record review.
• MRTA marijuana: automatic; an optional request to OCA to destroy the records.

AGENT GUIDANCE
For a 160.59 petition, the Certificate of Disposition is the keystone attachment — the application cannot
be completed without it, and a separate one is needed per case. Have the user request it first.
Where relief is automatic (160.50/160.55/Clean Slate/MRTA), there is nothing to file; direct the user to
verify status through a DCJS record review rather than preparing a motion.

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
The CPL 160.59 discretionary-sealing sequence (the main by-motion pathway):

1 Confirm eligibility: 10 years since sentencing or release, no more than two convictions and one felony, no
pending charges, and each offense is an “eligible offense” (not on the Section 3 list).

2 Obtain a Certificate of Disposition from the court for each case to be sealed (fee: $5 outside NYC, $10
within the five boroughs). Submit a separate request per case.

3 Complete the Sealing Application (Notice of Motion and Affidavit in Support), stating the convictions and
the reasons for sealing, and sign it before a notary public.

4 File the application in the court of conviction — for multiple eligible offenses, the court where the most
serious offense was decided (or, if equal class, where last convicted).
5 Serve the District Attorney for that county; the DA may consent or object. A sealing unit (e.g., in Brooklyn)
may help facilitate unopposed applications.

6 Court review / hearing. If the DA objects, the court holds a hearing; otherwise it may decide on the papers,
weighing rehabilitation and the interests of justice.

7 Order. If granted, the court orders sealing; DCJS and the courts seal the record from public and most private
access while retaining the law-enforcement and licensing exceptions.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Venue is offense-driven: file where the most serious offense was decided, or, for same-class offenses,
where the person was last convicted. Pin that down before filing.
Encourage the user to file 160.59 now rather than wait for Clean Slate if they meet the 10-year rule — the
automatic back-catalog may not reach them until 2027.

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• No true expungement (except marijuana). NY “clears” records by sealing; only MRTA marijuana
convictions are destroyed. Always use the accurate word with users.
• Clean Slate is phasing in. Automatic sealing took effect 11/16/2024 but OCA/DCJS have until 11/16/2027
to process the back-catalog — a record that should auto-seal may not be sealed yet; a person can petition for
review if it is overdue.
• Two parallel conviction routes. Clean Slate (automatic, 3/8 yr) and 160.59 (petition, 10 yr) coexist; the
petition is the way to seal sooner or to reach offenses outside Clean Slate.
• Certificate of Disposition is gatekeeping. The 160.59 application is built around it; the court fee is $5
outside NYC / $10 in the five boroughs, per case.
• County DA sealing units. Some offices (e.g., Brooklyn) actively facilitate 160.59 applications and publish
the ineligible-offense list in the form instructions.
• Sealed still counts as a prior. Under 160.59(10) a sealed conviction is still a predicate for sentence
enhancement in a later case.
• Class A felony split for Clean Slate. Non-drug Class A felonies are excluded, but Article 220 drug Class A
felonies can qualify — a frequently misunderstood point.

AGENT GUIDANCE
When a user believes Clean Slate already cleared their record, have them verify via a DCJS record
review before relying on it — implementation runs to 2027.
Lead the 160.59 process with the Certificate of Disposition; nothing else can be completed without it.

New York Record Sealing & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference ·10

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Amount Note

Certificate of Disposition (outside NYC) $5 per case Required for each 160.59 case

Certificate of Disposition (within NYC) $10 per case Five boroughs

CPL 160.59 sealing application No separate filing fee Court motion; notarization needed

Automatic sealing (160.50/160.55/Clean Slate) $0 No filing

MRTA marijuana destruction $0 Automatic; optional OCA request

DCJS record review (your own record) DCJS fee To confirm record / sealing status

Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; legal-aid sealing help available

New York is inexpensive to navigate. Automatic sealing and marijuana destruction cost nothing, and the 160.59
motion itself carries no separate filing fee — the only near-certain costs are the Certificate of Disposition ($5
outside NYC, $10 in the five boroughs, per case) and any DCJS record-review fee. Legal-aid organizations and
county DA sealing units assist pro se applicants at no cost.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Quote the $5/$10 Certificate of Disposition fee per case and note the 160.59 motion has no separate
filing fee — cost is rarely the barrier in NY. Point users to legal aid or a county DA sealing unit for free
help.
Lead with the free automatic routes (160.50/160.55/Clean Slate/MRTA) and have users verify status via
DCJS before spending anything.

sourceSections.title: NON-CONVICTION SEALING (CPL 160.50):
sourceSections.text:
A charge dismissed or resulting in acquittal is sealed
automatically at disposition. No application; confirm via DCJS.

sourceSections.title: CLEAN SLATE AUTOMATIC SEALING (CPL 160.57):
sourceSections.text:
An eligible misdemeanor conviction seals automatically 3 years
after sentencing/release (8 years for an eligible felony), with
no pending charges. OCA/DCJS process the back-catalog through
11/16/2027; verify status via a DCJS Record Review. If overdue,
petition the court for review.

sourceSections.title: MARIJUANA DESTRUCTION (MRTA):
sourceSections.text:
Convictions for unlawful possession and 5th-degree possession
of marijuana are expunged/destroyed automatically. An optional
request to OCA can confirm destruction. These do NOT count
toward the 160.59 two-conviction limit.

Proposed Order (petition pathway)
A 160.59 application is decided by court order. If granted, the order directs DCJS and the courts to seal the
specified conviction(s) from public and most private access, while preserving access for law enforcement,
